title: What is reality and how have we escaped from it
display_title: What is reality and how have we escaped from it
wikitext:
[]: What is reality and how have we escaped from it.             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Mon Dec 03, 2007 1:20 pm The end of evangelion is a beautiful movie, I don't think anyone here will argue that. The question that derives from that statement is, is it nothing more than an escape from a harsh reality? The escape from reality is something that's addressed several times throughout the series. Shinji's running away from his father and frequent refusal to pilot are considered "running away". Misato accuses the men in her life, mainly her father and Kaji, from escaping to their jobs in order to, "escape from a harsh reality and leave me behind." Which makes one wonder, are all jobs an escape, and if so an escape from what, why does Anno regret his profession so much if all jobs are equaly escapeist. At the end of End of Eva, Shinji has a discussion with Kaworu, Rei, and Asuka, who is clearly the most life affirming, on dreams, escape and reality. The cut to the movie theater in this scene is no doubt intended to turn the whole thing back on the viewer and tell them that this is only a dream, not even their own dream, but the dream of Anno, who has frequently criticized the Otaku subculture in Japan as escapist. So that forces several questions upon us as the viewer. As the series, and much of postmodern philosophy, tells us, the truth is subjective, reality is exclusive to the individual, as Jung puts it, "the individual is the only true reality"; or, as Rei says, "truth exists within your heart". So with that in mind, what is reality, and what are we escaping from? If eva strikes a cord within our hearts, capable of making us feel great pain and happiness, is still not real, then what role does it play in reality? If our dreams are an extension of reality, how are they to be treated in waking consciousness, and what of the dreams of others? In essence, what is it Anno accuses us of running away from, what is the reality that ought to be affirmed?

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Mon Dec 03, 2007 1:37 pm Very good points.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Dec 03, 2007 2:08 pm This is where I think Gendo's explanation of Instrumentality in EoTV actually fits in, an escape from isolation, from being trapped inside one's own skin.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Mon Dec 03, 2007 2:38 pm chee wrote: Very good points.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Dec 03, 2007 2:54 pm Squirrel Ninja wrote: to be like Asuka, or as Nitche puts it to give a resounding Yay to life. That is what I'm trying to get at.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Mon Dec 03, 2007 2:57 pm @ Squirrel Ninja: What Tines means by the above is how Asuka may or may not have tried to kill herself in Episode 24'. Also, Tines: Thanks for mentioning that viewpoint of yours with the Final Scene is your own little idiosyncrasy.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Mon Dec 03, 2007 3:22 pm Noted thanks. I don't think, for the record, that Asuka did try to kill herself in 24. For the exact reason that she strongly did not want to die. At that time though you are right, she wasn't life affirming at all. She was in many waysdead, and that death, the death of her ego/persona (which is similar to Shinji's crucifixion) was necessary for her ultimate rebirth in EoE (the pink cross) in which she blissfully fights in the full throw of life. In regards to EoE I think she was disgusted with Shinji, but I don't think she wanted him to kill her, but was more disgusted with his weakness, which the attempt to kill her in itself (killing Asuka is killing life for him, she is his only tie to emotion, bliss, beauty killing her would be exactly the denial of life that he is trying to overcome) seems to me to be a greater weakness than crying about it. IE she's disgusted with his reaction not his action, the mere fact that she is their at all reaffirms her new found identity and will to go on living. I think the EoE movie poster, in which Shinji and Asuka sit near the sea of LCL affirms this notion and offers a sort of hopeful resolution.

[]:             Quote Post by Defectron » Mon Dec 03, 2007 3:48 pm Which makes one wonder, are all jobs an escape, and if so an escape from what

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Dec 03, 2007 4:17 pm Very good points and even better questions. External reality is what it is. How we perceive that reality is all we have control over. Concepts like Instrumentality seems to be a metaphoric representation of what we're all seeking. Some kind of connection and completion through others that will allow us to escape a solitary, often lonely, "individual" existence. A way of looking at it is that with this individuality comes an existence where people are inside themselves. This nature creates a very real barrier between them and objective reality, as well as others' subjective reality (AT Field). So when we attempt to escape it's rather like trying to claw oneself out of that inner shell. When we experience something like NGE - and especially if we connect to it on a personal level - it's very much like we're making a real connection with another person, in this case, it's the artist. And in that connection, it's that sense that "You Are (Not) Alone" that comes through. I'm not sure if Anno is requesting we affirm reality. But he seems to be asking that we embrace it and all its imperfections. He seems to highlight this contradictory nature of our existence extremely well. EoE I've always said ends on that precise note. Its presenting of a harsh reality, and sometimes all we have is the ephemeral touch of another's embrace to confirm that there's hope in the most hopeless situation.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:23 pm JRR Tolkien's "On Faerie Stories" made a great point I like to remember, in which he defends accusations that His work is “escapist” as saying that critics that say this fail to see that there is a division between two kinds of “Escapism”: the Flight of the Deserter, and the Escape of the Prisoner. Nonetheless, from a Socratic/Platonic perspective, it is indeed important to recognize that there is “Truth” and “Reality” out there and we must find it. Much has been made of the Plato vs. Aristotle divide that essentially Aristotle felt that what we can observe in the world is “Real” and “the Truth”, and through such observation we can arrive at rationally making broader principles about the world. Plato said that what we see isn’t “Real” – Allegory of the Cave – just shadows, and we should try to find Truth/Reality. The problem is that this has caused many to just lump Plato together with the Eastern philosophies that deny the reality of anything (Hinduism, etc. says all is Illusion). Not so with Plato. Plato said that what we see isn’t “Real” but the key distinction is that he DID believe there was an “echo” of “Truth/Reality” in the world around us. Again, if we’re just seeing shadows on the wall of the cave….they’re still shadows of real things, and thus we can try to discern the Real form of these things from their shadows. Thus we’ve got three competing systems: nothing is real and we’re all butterflies having a bad dream (Eastern philosophies), what we see is physically real, and if we don’t understand the interrelationships between these real things we can using our reason (Aristotle), and what we see isn’t real, but it is an echo of Truth/Reality, and using our reason we can try to discern what this perfect world of pure Truth is like (Plato). That said, I’m an adamant Aristotelian, reject the Neo-Platonisists’ half-mystical bull-plop, and don’t give a dingo’s kidney’s about it.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:27 pm To Defectron- I guess what I'm asking isn't dependent necessarily on the fact that our jobs are enjoyable, but more the question of if thats an escape what is the threat. On the second part your right, it's a question of how to embrace the present reality not wither it's "real" or not,I'm sure we could go on and on about that. To Yojimbo- I think you had an answer similar to mine. I don't think there can be a universal as to how to embrace reality by the very fact that reality itself is subjective. I think the fact that the experience is individual makes it that much more meaningful, it makes the triumph of forming a true meaningful connection, wither it is ourselves with Anno or Shinji with Asuka, that much more valuable. Would I be right on the assumption that your comment about inner shells meant that cutting ourselves off from felling, from the experience of other people or the things, like eva, that give us both pleasure and pain? Is that what it is to escape to find the pain of life out weighs the pleasure; to do what Shinji says about locking his heart away? That would be my opinion at least. I think affirming and embracing reality are the same thing, however I would argue that reality is in itself perfect, if their were no pain, it would loose something, it would become trivialized by that fact, and the comfort of others, that gentle caress would become meaningless.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:32 pm V wrote: critics that say this fail to see that there is a division between two kinds of “Escapism”: the Flight of the Deserter, and the Escape of the Prisoner.

[]:             Quote Post by Squirrel Ninja » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:37 pm To V- I would have to fight you on that point. I don't think truth is something that we have to find "out there" but rather something to be found "in here" within ourselves. If your talking about finding a universal truth, I don't think it exists, but I think each person has the potential to find a personal truth that for them justifies all of existence. This truth will never be rational, because rationality is a sociological ideal, and the inner-workings of the mind and body are inherently irrational. By claiming the world as an echo of truth, and the higher truth, what would be called God, is something other that has to be sought out, I would consider just as life-denying as calling it all a bad dream. If it is all a dream it is a good dream, and it ought to be accepted as real and be embraced, the question that goes right back to the source of the thread is how do we get at that, and what must be done to embrace it.

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:53 pm Squirrel Ninja wrote: The end of evangelion is a beautiful movie, I don't think anyone here will argue that. The question that derives from that statement is, is it nothing more than an escape from a harsh reality?

[]:             Quote Post by V » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:54 pm 1 - The world, and the objective truth of the world's physical existence, is indeed real. The physical world of matter, oceans, mountains, rocks, air, planets, etc. is "real" independent of humans. 2 - the problem is that so many focus on the "are things real without humans?" question, that it's really obscuring the real question: yes, everything is indeed "Truth" and "Real". The actual question is "Teleologically or Morally, are things real?" That is teleologically an apple is a thing for humans to eat, while empirically it is a fruit of a tree, made out of carbohydrates and amino acids, roughly 4-6 inches across, colored red. The real question isn't whether "the world is real". It is, that's a given. The question is "is the morality and meaning we assign the world universally true somehow, or is it nothing but the Rorschach-like values we have assigned to it?" "Truth" has nothing to do with "God". Well Aristotle thought so, but later luminaries did not; there can be a "universal "Truth"" behind everything the "Cosmic Watchmaker" of sorts....actually Aristotle "believed in God" in the "Cosmic Watchmaker" sense (which is neither Judeo-Christian or athiestic)...a universe of orderly rules operating on their own, we just don't understand them all yet...but we are capable of understanding them. That we physically exist is a fact. The question which this first one has obscured is "are our interpersonal and/or moral interactions "real""? I.e. within the show, Shinji and Asuka exist. Shinji could choose to live in the dream-world of Instrumentality where everything is happy, but he knows that as this is a product of his own mind it isn't actually "real". So he chooses the harsher and more flawed "true" emotional reactions that the real world Asuka offers. Which frankly I think was the smart choice. But we cannot just throw up our hands, despair of the whole endeavor, and just surrender to believing that we're just butterflies having a bad dream or something. There is such a thing as universal Truth (in the Immanuel Kant sense)...though whether subjective human moral constructs are "real"....they are indeed conceptual real if immaterial.

[]:             Quote Post by Themaninblack » Mon Dec 03, 2007 5:58 pm ...This disscussion is heading down a very dangrous path, a real dangrous one. Though we are still on topic.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Mon Dec 03, 2007 6:01 pm Anno's point was simply that otaku and anime nerds have to stop living in a dream-world of vicariously experiencing real life through television, movies, and the internet, and start developing our own social interactions and lives, and that, while it might be less painful to *mentally* live in such a dream world, the veracity of the flawed real world makes it by far the better choice. Otherwise we'll all turn into hikkikomori.

[]:             Quote Post by Themaninblack » Mon Dec 03, 2007 6:04 pm V wrote: Anno's point was simply that otaku and anime nerds have to stop living in a dream-world of vicariously experiencing real life through television, movies, and the internet, and start developing our own social interactions and lives, and that, while it might be less painful to *mentally* live in such a dream world, the veracity of the flawed real world makes it by far the better choice. Otherwise we'll all turn into hikkikomori.

[]:             Quote Post by Zuggy » Mon Dec 03, 2007 6:08 pm Themaninblack wrote: Though That was not what I was getting at.

[]:             Quote Post by Themaninblack » Mon Dec 03, 2007 6:10 pm Zugzwang wrote: Themaninblack wrote: Though That was not what I was getting at.
